flashcards.definition: A measurable property of a physical system (e.g., mass, length, time).
One of the seven fundamental quantities in SI: length, mass, time, current, temperature, amount of substance, luminous intensity.
A quantity formed from combinations of base quantities (e.g., velocity = length/time).
A standard used to measure a physical quantity (e.g., meter, kilogram, second).
The process of comparing a physical quantity to a standard unit.
The International System of Units — the globally accepted metric system with seven base units.
A multiplier attached to a unit (e.g., kilo- = 10³, milli- = 10⁻³, nano- = 10⁻⁹).
The SI base unit of length.
The SI base unit of mass.
The SI base unit of time.
A quantity with magnitude only (e.g., speed, mass, temperature).
A quantity with both magnitude and direction (e.g., velocity, force, displacement).
The size or amount of a quantity.
The single vector that has the same effect as two or more vectors combined.
Vectors are added tip-to-tail; the resultant goes from the start of the first to the end of the last.
How close a measurement is to the true value.
How close repeated measurements are to each other.
All reliably known digits plus one estimated digit in a measurement.
A consistent error in one direction; affects accuracy.
Irregular fluctuations in measurements; affects precision.
Using units to check equations and convert between unit systems.
The physical nature of a quantity expressed in terms of base quantities (e.g., [L], [M], [T]).
A ratio equal to 1 used to convert units (e.g., 1 km / 1000 m).
Both sides of an equation must have the same dimensions.
Multiplying by conversion factors so unwanted units cancel.
The margin of error in a measurement, in the same units as the measurement.
(absolute uncertainty / measured value) × 100%.
Rules for combining uncertainties: add for sums/differences; add percentages for products/quotients.
Evaluating how measurement uncertainties affect calculated results.
|experimental − accepted| / accepted × 100%.
A fundamental property of matter; positive (+) or negative (−).
F = kq₁q₂/r²; force between two point charges.
e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C; charge of a proton (electron = −e).
Friction, conduction, and induction.
Total charge in an isolated system remains constant.
E = F/q = kQ/r²; force per unit positive charge.
Point from positive to negative; density indicates field strength.
V = kQ/r; energy per unit charge. Units: volts (V = J/C).
ΔV = W/q; work done per unit charge to move between two points.
Lines of constant potential; perpendicular to field lines.
C = Q/V; ability to store charge. Units: farads (F).
C = ε₀A/d; capacitance depends on area, distance, and dielectric.
Insulating material between plates that increases capacitance.
U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/(2C).
Series: 1/C_total = Σ(1/Cᵢ). Parallel: C_total = ΣCᵢ.
I = Q/t; flow of charge per unit time. Units: amperes (A).
R = V/I; opposition to current flow. Units: ohms (Ω).
V = IR; voltage equals current × resistance.
ρ; a material property. R = ρL/A.
Conductors: low resistance (metals). Insulators: high resistance (rubber, glass).
Components connected end-to-end; same current through all. R_total = ΣRᵢ.
Components connected across each other; same voltage. 1/R_total = Σ(1/Rᵢ).
Total current into a junction = total current out (conservation of charge).
Sum of voltage gains and drops around any closed loop = 0.
P = IV = I²R = V²/R.
A field produced by moving charges or magnets; units: tesla (T).
F = qvB sin θ; perpendicular to both v and B.
F = BIL sin θ.
Fingers point in current direction, curl toward B → thumb gives force direction.
B = μ₀I/(2πr); circles around the wire.
A changing magnetic flux induces an EMF (voltage) in a conductor.
EMF = −dΦ_B/dt; induced EMF equals the rate of change of magnetic flux.
Φ_B = BA cos θ; the product of field, area, and the cosine of the angle.
The induced current flows in a direction to oppose the change in flux.
Generators, transformers, induction cooktops.
Current that periodically reverses direction; V = V₀ sin(ωt).
V_rms = V₀/√2, I_rms = I₀/√2; effective values for power calculations.
Transfers AC between circuits; V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂ (turns ratio).
The total opposition to AC; combines resistance, capacitive, and inductive reactance.
f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)); where impedance is minimized in an RLC circuit.
Four fundamental equations unifying electricity, magnetism, and light.
∮E·dA = Q_enc/ε₀; electric flux through a closed surface ∝ enclosed charge.
∮B·dA = 0; no magnetic monopoles — field lines form closed loops.
∮E·dl = −dΦ_B/dt; changing magnetic flux creates an electric field.
Self-propagating oscillation of electric and magnetic fields; speed = c.
Electrons are emitted from a metal surface when struck by light of sufficient frequency.
E = hf; energy of a photon depends on frequency, not intensity.
Minimum energy needed to eject an electron from a surface.
Minimum frequency for photoemission: f₀ = φ/h.
KE_max = hf − φ; kinetic energy of emitted electrons.
'Plum pudding' — positive charge with electrons embedded.
Dense positive nucleus with electrons orbiting (discovered via gold foil experiment).
Electrons orbit in quantized energy levels; emit photons when jumping down.
Eₙ = −13.6/n² eV for hydrogen; quantized, discrete values.
Discrete wavelengths emitted by excited atoms — unique to each element.
Unstable nuclei emit radiation (alpha, beta, or gamma) to become more stable.
Time for half the atoms in a sample to decay: N = N₀(½)^(t/t₁/₂).
A heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei, releasing energy (e.g., uranium-235).
Light nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy (e.g., hydrogen → helium).
E = mc²; mass can be converted to energy.
Einstein's theory: laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames; c is constant.
Moving clocks run slower: Δt = γΔt₀ where γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²).
Moving objects are shorter in the direction of motion: L = L₀/γ.
γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²); approaches infinity as v → c.
A traveling twin ages less than the stationary twin due to time dilation.
A mathematical function describing the quantum state of a particle.
Δx · Δp ≥ ℏ/2; position and momentum cannot both be precisely known.
|ψ|² gives the probability of finding a particle at a particular location.
Energy, angular momentum, and other quantities come in discrete packets.
The fundamental equation governing quantum mechanics; predicts wave functions.
The theory classifying all known fundamental particles and three of four forces.
Fundamental particles that make up protons and neutrons (6 flavors: up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom).
Fundamental particles including electrons, muons, taus, and their neutrinos.
Force-carrying particles: photon (EM), gluon (strong), W/Z (weak), Higgs.
Particles with the same mass but opposite charge; annihilate with matter releasing energy.
The total path length traveled; a scalar quantity.
The straight-line change in position from start to end; a vector.
The rate of distance traveled: speed = distance / time; a scalar.
The rate of displacement: v = Δx / Δt; a vector.
Average is over an interval; instantaneous is at a specific moment.
The rate of change of velocity: a = Δv / Δt; a vector (m/s²).
Constant acceleration — velocity changes at a steady rate.
Acceleration opposite to the direction of motion (slowing down).
Points in the direction of the change in velocity.
Acceleration at a specific instant; slope of v-t graph at a point.
Slope = velocity. Straight line = constant velocity; curve = acceleration.
Slope = acceleration. Area under curve = displacement.
Area under curve = change in velocity.
Horizontal line on v-t graph; straight diagonal on x-t graph.
Curved lines on x-t or v-t graphs indicate changing velocity or acceleration.
Final velocity = initial velocity + (acceleration × time).
Displacement = initial velocity × time + half acceleration × time².
Relates velocity, acceleration, and displacement without time.
Displacement = average velocity × time.
These kinematic equations only apply when acceleration is constant.
Motion under gravity alone; a = g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward.
Motion in two dimensions under gravity: horizontal (constant v) and vertical (free fall).
The horizontal distance a projectile travels; max at 45° launch angle.
The parabolic path of a projectile (ignoring air resistance).
Horizontal and vertical motions are analyzed independently.
A coordinate system used to describe motion; can be stationary or moving.
The velocity of one object as observed from another: v_AB = v_A − v_B.
A reference frame not accelerating — Newton's laws hold in their simplest form.
An accelerating reference frame where fictitious forces appear.
Position and velocity transformations between frames moving at constant relative velocity.
A push or pull on an object; a vector measured in newtons (N).
A force requiring physical contact (normal, friction, tension, applied).
A force acting at a distance (gravity, electric, magnetic).
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines acceleration.
A diagram showing all forces acting on a single object as vectors.
An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted on by a net force.
The tendency of an object to resist changes in its motion; proportional to mass.
Net force = 0; object is at rest or moving at constant velocity.
A measure of inertia; the quantity of matter in an object (kg).
Forces that are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction; produce no acceleration.
F_net = ma; acceleration is proportional to net force and inversely proportional to mass.
The SI unit of force: 1 N = 1 kg · m/s².
The gravitational force on an object: W = mg.
Mass is intrinsic (kg); weight depends on gravitational field (N).
Always in the direction of the net force.
For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force.
Two forces that are equal, opposite, and act on different objects.
The perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object.
Action and reaction forces never cancel because they act on different objects.
Walking: foot pushes ground backward, ground pushes foot forward.
A force opposing the relative motion or tendency of motion between surfaces.
Prevents motion; f_s ≤ μ_s N (up to a maximum value).
Opposes sliding motion; f_k = μ_k N (constant).
A dimensionless ratio depending on the surfaces; μ_s > μ_k.
The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable.
Perpendicular to the surface; balances the component of weight into the surface.
A flat surface tilted at angle θ; weight splits into parallel (mg sin θ) and perpendicular (mg cos θ) components.
mg sin θ — pulls the object down the ramp.
mg cos θ — pushes the object into the surface; equals normal force.
Object is static when friction ≥ mg sin θ.
Motion along a circular path at constant speed; direction constantly changes.
a_c = v²/r; directed toward the center of the circle.
F_c = mv²/r; the net inward force causing circular motion.
Time for one complete revolution: T = 2πr/v.
A tilted road surface that provides a component of normal force for centripetal acceleration.
An accelerating reference frame where Newton's laws require fictitious forces.
A fictitious force (e.g., centrifugal) that appears in a non-inertial frame to account for acceleration.
The outward pseudo-force in a rotating frame; equals mv²/r directed away from center.
A pseudo-force that deflects moving objects in a rotating reference frame (e.g., Earth).
Weather patterns, Foucault pendulum, apparent forces in elevators.
W = Fd cos θ; energy transferred by a force acting over a displacement.
The SI unit of work/energy: 1 J = 1 N·m.
Force has a component in the direction of displacement (energy added).
Force opposes displacement (energy removed, e.g., friction).
Force is perpendicular to displacement (cos 90° = 0).
KE = ½mv²; energy of motion.
PE = mgh; energy due to position in a gravitational field.
PE = ½kx²; energy stored in a stretched or compressed spring.
The sum of kinetic and potential energy: E = KE + PE.
The position where gravitational PE is defined as zero.
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed.
KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂ (when only conservative forces act).
Conversion between forms (PE ↔ KE, chemical → thermal, etc.).
Non-conservative forces (friction) that convert mechanical energy to thermal energy.
Roller coasters, pendulums, falling objects.
P = W/t = Fv; the rate of doing work or transferring energy.
The SI unit of power: 1 W = 1 J/s.
η = (useful output / total input) × 100%; always less than 100% due to losses.
1 hp ≈ 746 W; a non-SI unit of power.
Comparing engines, motors, and energy use rates.
W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mv₀²; net work equals the change in kinetic energy.
The total work done by all forces on an object.
If net work is positive, KE increases (object speeds up); if negative, KE decreases.
Work = area under the force-displacement graph.
For non-constant forces, integrate F·dx or use area under F-x graph.
A force where work is path-independent (gravity, spring); energy is recoverable.
A force where work depends on the path (friction, air resistance); energy is dissipated.
Only conservative forces have associated potential energy functions.
Work done by a conservative force between two points is the same regardless of path.
Work done by a conservative force around a closed path is zero.
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass × velocity.
kg·m/s.
Same direction as velocity.
F = dp/dt; force is the rate of change of momentum.
Requires large mass, large velocity, or both.
J = FΔt = Δp; the product of force and time interval, equal to the change in momentum.
The impulse on an object equals the change in its momentum.
Longer collision time reduces the average force (airbags, crumple zones).
Equals impulse.
Impulse has units of N·s = kg·m/s.
In an isolated system, total momentum before = total momentum after.
No external forces act on the system.
Σp_before = Σp_after.
Momentum is conserved in each direction independently.
Collisions, explosions, recoil.
Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved; objects bounce apart.
Momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not; some KE is lost to heat/deformation.
Objects stick together after collision; maximum KE loss.
e = (separation speed) / (approach speed); e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic.
In 2D, conserve momentum separately in x and y directions.
The average position of all mass in a system: x_cm = Σmᵢxᵢ / Σmᵢ.
Moves as if all mass were concentrated there with all external forces applied.
Center of mass is at the geometric center for uniform, symmetric objects.
Do not affect the motion of the center of mass.
Thrown wrench rotates around its center of mass while flying through the air.
Thrust comes from expelling mass (exhaust) — conservation of momentum.
Δv = v_e · ln(m₀/m_f); relates speed change to exhaust velocity and mass ratio.
F = v_e · (dm/dt); force equals exhaust velocity × mass flow rate.
m₀/m_f; ratio of initial to final mass — larger ratio means greater Δv.
Space travel: no air to push against, so momentum of exhaust provides thrust.
F = Gm₁m₂/r²; the gravitational force between two masses.
G ≈ 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Gravitational force decreases with the square of distance.
Gravity acts between all objects with mass.
Gravitational forces from multiple sources add as vectors.
g = F/m = GM/r²; the force per unit mass at a point in space.
Point toward the mass; density indicates field strength.
g = GM/R² where R is the planet's radius; ≈ 9.8 m/s² on Earth.
Zero inside a uniform spherical shell (Shell Theorem).
Decreases with altitude; differs slightly across Earth's surface.
An object in free fall around a massive body; gravity provides centripetal force.
v = √(GM/r); speed for a circular orbit at distance r.
T = 2πr/v = 2π√(r³/GM).
In orbit, objects are in free fall together — no normal force, apparent weight = 0.
Orbit with T = 24 hours, directly above the equator at ~35,786 km altitude.
An object orbiting a larger body due to gravity.
Altitude ~200–2,000 km; period ~90 minutes.
Fixed position above the equator; used for communications.
Passes over both poles; used for Earth observation.
E_total = −GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits.
v_e = √(2GM/R); minimum speed to escape a gravitational field.
Escape velocity doesn't depend on the mass of the escaping object.
≈ 11.2 km/s from the surface.
U = −GMm/r; zero at infinity, negative when bound.
Total energy < 0: bound orbit. Total energy ≥ 0: escapes.
Planets orbit in ellipses with the Sun at one focus.
A line from Sun to planet sweeps equal areas in equal times (faster near Sun).
T² ∝ r³; for the Sun's planets: T² = (4π²/GM)r³.
An oval shape with two foci; eccentricity measures elongation.
Closest / farthest point to the Sun in an orbit.
Oscillation where the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = −kx.
A force directed toward the equilibrium position.
The position where the net force is zero.
Repetitive back-and-forth motion about an equilibrium.
A classic SHM example: mass on a spring with F = −kx.
Time for one complete oscillation; measured in seconds.
Number of oscillations per second: f = 1/T; measured in hertz (Hz).
Maximum displacement from equilibrium.
ω = 2πf = 2π/T; measured in rad/s.
T = 2π√(L/g); depends on length and gravity, not mass.
Total energy E = ½kA²; constant, alternating between KE and PE.
KE is maximum, PE is zero, speed is maximum.
PE is maximum, KE is zero, speed is zero.
½kx² + ½mv² = ½kA² at every point.
SHM with friction — amplitude gradually decreases over time.
A disturbance that transfers energy without transferring matter.
The distance between consecutive identical points on a wave (e.g., crest to crest).
v = fλ; frequency × wavelength.
Particle displacement is perpendicular to wave direction (e.g., light, string waves).
Particle displacement is parallel to wave direction (e.g., sound).
When waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements.
Waves combine in phase — amplitudes add; larger resultant.
Waves combine out of phase — amplitudes subtract; smaller or zero resultant.
The difference in distance traveled by two waves; determines constructive or destructive.
The position in a wave cycle; in phase = same point in cycle.
A wave pattern that appears stationary, formed by superposition of two traveling waves.
A point of zero displacement in a standing wave.
A point of maximum displacement in a standing wave.
Occurs when a driving frequency matches a natural frequency — maximum amplitude.
Integer multiples of the fundamental frequency: fₙ = nf₁.
The apparent change in frequency of a wave due to relative motion between source and observer.
Frequency increases (higher pitch for sound, blueshift for light).
Frequency decreases (lower pitch, redshift).
f' = f × (v ± v_o) / (v ∓ v_s); signs depend on motion direction.
Radar guns, medical ultrasound, redshift of galaxies.
Light and matter exhibit both wave and particle properties.
A quantum (packet) of light energy: E = hf.
λ = h/p = h/(mv); wavelength associated with a moving particle.
h ≈ 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s; fundamental constant linking energy and frequency.
Demonstrates wave-like interference for light and even electrons.
A longitudinal mechanical wave that travels through a medium.
A region of high pressure in a sound wave.
A region of low pressure in a sound wave.
Sound requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) — cannot travel through a vacuum.
A wave that requires matter to propagate.
≈ 343 m/s at 20°C; increases with temperature.
Fastest in solids, slowest in gases (denser, stiffer media → faster).
v ≈ 331 + 0.6T (m/s) where T is in °C.
Ratio of object's speed to speed of sound: Mach 1 = speed of sound.
Shock wave produced when an object exceeds the speed of sound.
Power per unit area: I = P/A; measured in W/m².
The perceived volume of sound; related to intensity (subjective).
Sound level: β = 10 log(I/I₀); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of hearing).
Sound intensity decreases as 1/r² with distance from the source.
≈ 120 dB; the intensity at which sound becomes painful.
The perceived highness or lowness of a sound; determined by frequency.
Humans hear approximately 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz.
Frequencies below 20 Hz; below human hearing range.
Frequencies above 20,000 Hz; used in medical imaging.
The lowest natural frequency of a vibrating object.
Standing waves form in pipes; resonant frequencies depend on pipe length.
Both ends open; all harmonics present. fₙ = nv/(2L).
One end closed; only odd harmonics. fₙ = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,...
Closed end = node; open end = antinode.
Musical instruments: flutes, organs, brass instruments.
Periodic fluctuations in loudness when two close frequencies interfere.
f_beat = |f₁ − f₂|.
Integer multiples of the fundamental: 1st harmonic = fundamental, 2nd = first overtone.
Any resonant frequency above the fundamental.
The quality of sound determined by the mix of harmonics (distinguishes instruments).
Light bounces off a surface; angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
Light bends when passing between media with different optical densities.
n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles and indices of refraction.
n = c/v; ratio of light speed in vacuum to speed in the medium.
A line perpendicular to the surface at the point of incidence.
Converges light; produces real or virtual images depending on object distance.
Diverges light; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images.
Thicker in the middle; converges light to a focal point.
Thinner in the middle; diverges light away from the focal axis.
1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i; relates focal length, object distance, and image distance.
Light reflects entirely within a medium when the angle exceeds the critical angle.
θ_c = sin⁻¹(n₂/n₁); exists only when light moves to a less dense medium.
Uses total internal reflection to transmit light through thin glass fibers.
High index of refraction → small critical angle → many internal reflections.
Telecommunications, endoscopy, decorative lighting.
Uses two converging lenses to magnify small objects.
Uses lenses or mirrors to view distant objects.
The ratio of image size to object size: M = −d_i/d_o.
A converging lens forms a real image on film or a sensor.
A lens system that focuses images on the retina; accommodates by changing lens shape.
The bending of light around obstacles or through narrow openings.
Central bright maximum with smaller maxima on either side.
Produces alternating bright and dark fringes: dsinθ = mλ.
Many slits that produce sharp, bright fringes for precise wavelength measurement.
Colorful patterns from constructive/destructive interference in thin layers.
Restricting light oscillation to one plane.
Light vibrating in all directions perpendicular to propagation.
A filter that transmits light oscillating in one direction.
I = I₀ cos²θ; intensity after passing through a second polarizer at angle θ.
At Brewster's angle, reflected light is fully polarized.
